content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

June 17, 1993

Charles Bell
Bell, McAndrews & Hiltachk
555 Capitol Mall, Suite 530
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-93-234

Dear Mr. Bell:

I am writing regarding your request for advice concerning
Government Code Section 85305 of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act"). You inquired as to whether a contributor may make, and a
special election candidate may accept, contributions outside the
"special election cycle" or the "special runoff election cycle,"
if the funds are used for retirement of debts incurred by the
candidate's committee for such periods.

Specifically you asked whether an individual may, during a
special runoff election cycle, contribute $1,000 to a candidate
committee to help retire special election debt and, during the same
period, contribute an additional $1,000 to the candidate's
special runoff election.

Section 85305 provides that the contribution limitations
shall apply during special election cycles and special runoff
election cycles, as follows:

(c)(1) No person shall make, and no candidate
for elective office, or campaign treasurer, shall
solicit or accept any contribution or loan which
would cause the total amount contributed or loaned
by that person to that candidate, including
contributions or loans to all committees controlled
by the candidate, to exceed one thousand dollars

1 Government Code Sections 81000-91015. All statutory
references are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations
Section 18000 et seq. All references to regulations are to
Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento, CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

File No. A-93-234
Page 2


($1,000) during any special election cycle or
special runoff election cycle.

Section 85305(c)(1).

Section 85305 specifically delineates the time period
during which a contribution may be given and accepted. "Special
election cycle" means the day on which the office becomes vacant
until the day of the special election. "Special runoff election
cycle" means the day after the special election until the day of
the special runoff election. (Section 85305(b)(1) and (2).)

Accordingly, an individual may contribute $1,000 during the
special election cycle and $1,000 during the special runoff
election cycle. In your example, the individual would be
contributing $2,000 during the "special runoff election cycle."
This is not allowable under the statute.

The Commission adopted Regulation 18535, which sets forth
guidelines for contribution limitations during special and special
runoff elections. Subdivision (d) of this regulation provides:

(d) Following the date of the special
election or special runoff election, a candidate
for or campaign treasurer of a candidate for that
special election or special runoff election, may do
the following:

(1) Solicit and accept contributions for the
special election committee subject to the
limitations set forth in Government Code Section
85305.

(2) Transfer contributions to the special
election committee so long as the contributions are
transferred pursuant to subdivisions (a), (b), and
(c).

Subdivision (d) was adopted to allow an individual to
contribute to a candidate's committee to help retire the debt
after the election, if the individual did not contribute up to the
limits during the special election cycles. For example, a
candidate who successfully ran in both the special election and
the special runoff election could accept a $2,000 contribution
after the special runoff election from an individual who did not
contribute during either election cycle.

File No. A-93-234
Page 3


I trust this answers your question.

Sincerely,

Jeff Marschner
General Counsel

By: Jill Stecher
Counsel, Legal Division

JM:JS:ak

sections.question: Whether a contributor may make, and a special election candidate may accept, contributions outside the "special election cycle" or the "special runoff election cycle," if the funds are used for retirement of debts incurred by the candidate's committee for such periods. Specifically, whether an individual may, during a special runoff election cycle, contribute $1,000 to a candidate committee to help retire special election debt and, during the same period, contribute an additional $1,000 to the candidate's special runoff election.
sections.conclusion: An individual may contribute $1,000 during the special election cycle and $1,000 during the special runoff election cycle. In the example provided, the individual would be contributing $2,000 during the "special runoff election cycle," which is not allowable under the statute. However, under Regulation 18535(d), after the special runoff election, a candidate may solicit and accept contributions to retire election debts from individuals who did not contribute during the election cycles, subject to the limitations in Government Code Section 85305.
sections.question_synthetic: May a contributor make separate $1,000 contributions during a special runoff election cycle—one for retiring special election debt and one for the special runoff election itself—or are these contributions subject to an aggregate limit?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The proposed contributions are not allowable during the special runoff election cycle, as they would exceed the $1,000 limit for that period. However, contributions to retire campaign debt may be accepted after the election concludes, subject to Government Code Section 85305 limitations, from individuals who did not contribute during the election cycles.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Standard FPPC advice letter format with clear Question and Conclusion sections. File number A-93-234 corresponds to document ID 93-234. Legal analysis includes statutory citations and regulatory interpretation.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether a contributor may make, and a special election candidate may accept, contributions outside the "special election cycle" or the "special runoff election cycle," if the funds are used for retirement of debts incurred by the candidate's committee for such periods. Specifically, whether an individual may, during a special runoff election cycle, contribute $1,000 to a candidate committee to help retire special election debt and, during the same period, contribute an additional $1,000 to the candidate's special runoff election.

CONCLUSION: An individual may contribute $1,000 during the special election cycle and $1,000 during the special runoff election cycle. In the example provided, the individual would be contributing $2,000 during the "special runoff election cycle," which is not allowable under the statute. However, under Regulation 18535(d), after the special runoff election, a candidate may solicit and accept contributions to retire election debts from individuals who did not contribute during the election cycles, subject to the limitations in Government Code Section 85305.

embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter addressing whether contributions to retire campaign debt can be accepted outside election cycles. The Commission concludes that separate $1,000 contributions during the same election cycle violate contribution limits, but post-election debt retirement contributions are permissible under Regulation 18535(d) for contributors who did not max out during the election.
